speaker: interviewer
text: Four, an interview with Olga Drucker in Merrick, New York, on March 25, 1996.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of the Wooley family when I was still with them. I was with them for one year, from 1944 to 1945. I was working there as a mother's helper, and as you can see, there are a lot of children. They're not all the Wooley children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's see. Mr. and Mrs. Wooley are each holding one of the twins, Edward and [? Francis. ?] And in the back row are a few American, and I think British, armed services men who used to come to their house on their leaves. And I fell in love about every other week with somebody or other. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the boy on the right is their oldest son, Alexander, and then there's Margaret Alice and Caroline and Jane. That's all their children. And then the other two girls [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and the woman that's holding another little girl, they were all somehow living in the house, evacuees or whatever. They had a huge house, and they always took people in. And I'm the one all the way on the right. And as you can see, I'm now 16 years old and a little chubby. And I'm well-fed on-- on English starches and Russian food. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And as you can see, here we are in America. This is the Statue of Liberty, and I'm the one on the left. And the one on the right is my cousin, who's a little older than I am, Hilda, who-- she and I le-- left Stuttgart together on the same Kindertransport. She was in England only less than a year, and then she and her family went to America. She was-- her parents were already in America, and her story is different. What isn't different, though, is that she and I are both invited back to Stuttgart the summer of 1996 by invitation from the mayor of Stuttgart for former Jewish citizens who will be shown around and-- and be treated to quite a number of events for two weeks. I'm also going to Germany at that time, on a book tour, for my book called which was published in Germany. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] When my husband and I went back to England in 1963, one of the times we went back there, we visited Lonsdale-- Lonsdale House School, it was called in those days. This is the boarding school that I went to in Norwich, when I-- my first year in England, where I first learned English. And Miss Callis was the headmistress at that time. And it was her brother, Mr. Callis and his family, that I lived with in Wellingborough. So this was the beginning. And in fact, Miss Callis, who was the headmistress of this school, told-- wrote to my parents when war was about started, or about to start, that if anything should happen to them, she would be my-- she would want to be my legal guardian. So I have great connections to Lonsdale School.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well now, here we have a picture taken in 1989, which-- Olga is all grown up now, and the woman with her is Mrs. Wooley, Mariana Wooley, the mother of all those children. And we're there because there was a reu-- a 50th reunion of the Kindertransport. And there were, I think, about 2,000 people that came from all over the world to this reunion. And from America, about 200, and I was one of them. And I invited-- I had permission to invite-- my foster mother. And she and I, in fact, gave a little concert at the reunion. I played the cello and she played the flute. And that-- that is the reunion where my book was conceived. I realized then and there that I had to write about this experience. And I did. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, Olga Levy Drucker. I-- I took it upon myself to take my-- my original name back. I have no idea what my father would have said about that, but he might have been happy about it. And this is the cover, of course, of the book called It's a book for children, although I understand that adults like it too. It's-- it's doing very well, I thank you. It's in its third edition of the hardback cover. It's out in soft back. It's being distributed by Scholastic in American schools. It's been published in German, translated in Germany and in Holland. And this is the book I'm going to go to Germany about to promote it. And I go to schools and to different organizations and talk about the Kindertransport. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] How young we were. This is our wedding photo, Rolf and yours truly. And the rest is history. We were married in 1950. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And now we jump ahead 41 years, and this is 1991 at our dau-- our youngest daughter's wedding. That the bride is Alice, and she married Tom Nicholl, who came over from Ireland to marry her. And his Irish-- let's see now. Let me go from left to right. The left, the man is Tom's brother, Brian Nicholl. And then there's my sister-in-law, my brother's wife, Ellen. And then third one is Jane, our oldest daughter. And then it's Rolf, Alice and Tom, and me. And that's Robert, our son, to my-- on the right side. And [? Una, ?] from Ireland, who is Brian's wife. I'm going to give you a test after this. And I don't know who the man is at the-- oh, that's my brother. My brother Herbert. And the little girl is the flower girl. Her name is Erica Hor-- Horton, and she's a cousin. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And this is Andrew Thomas Nicholl, my three and-- three-year-old grandson and apple of my eye. He is Tom and Alice's son, and we're very proud of him. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] In 1979, a poem of mine was published in an American anthology about America, by the American Poetry Society. This won an award, and it's called 'America, My Home.' 'This is home. The other no longer exists, land of the goose-stepping boots, gas-ovens and vapor of burning flesh that lingers on to the end of days. Here is where I've spread my tender, bruised, and searching roots. Here stands my house, the rooms in which I've raised my children, taught them to sing, where they had their colds and stomach upsets; in which we have celebrated birthdays, anniversaries, Pesachs, Hannukahs, and have lighted the Sabbath candles. Here is where I have been free to love America, my home.' [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: With pleasure. This is Rolf Drucker, my husband of 45 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolf, is there anything you would like to add to this tape as the husband of a woman who was on the Kindertransport?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes. Olga has grown tremendously in-- in this Kindertransport project. At first, she had very little involvement in memorizing it. I guess she was busy with our family and with our lives. But after the Kindertransport reunion in London, she sudden-- and she wrote the book. She has become completely involved in this, and this has taken over a good part of her life. And she is very active and-- and very effective, I think, in maintaining that. And it's given our children, also, something. Our children, although we tried very hard to educate them as though they were just normal, average, American children, it seems in retrospect they all remember that their youth was quite different from that of their peers. And they very much feel the result of our having been involved in the-- in the Holocaust, And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we think-- the question is what do-- what lessons to draw from that. And I think the-- the lessons to draw is that each generation has to come to grips with itself. There are no definitive answers or definitive [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] finishes to this. It comes up again. It is almost impossible to bury. The Germans find it impossible to bury. We find it impossible to bury. And each generation has to come to grips with it again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and I think that what has to be stressed-- I would say to a young person today, oh, perhaps as young as nine, 10 years old, that you have to pay attention to history and to what has been, in order to know where to go in the future. You've got to look back in order to know what the front will hold. And you've got to respect one another and respect a person that is different. It's not-- it-- this is about tolerance, but it's-- it's more than that. It's about respect that we're all human beings on this earth, and-- and so we-- we must respect each other, one another. We're all God's children. And there's got to be a time when wars will be a thing of the absolutely past. Things-- differences cannot be solved anymore by-- by fighting and killing one another. I-- I wish that all guns and-- and military weapons would disappear off the face of the earth. I know I'm-- I'm not being realistic. I never was and never will be. But I do think that the beginning of something like this is to look at one another, try to understand one another, and-- and respect each other as human beings. And that's what I've always tried to teach our kids too, I think, and we both have. And if-- if they have, in their childhood, I-- I have heard them say that they-- they grew up expecting the bottom to fall out of their world. Well, I'm sorry if that's how they felt. It's-- it's-- I-- I can't-- I can't say it was anybody's fault, unless it was Hitler's fault. But now they have to get on with that-- and I think they're doing it-- and-- and just know that life can be beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for sharing your testimony and your story with us.
